content:
Evidence Was There All Along

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Clay Banks on UnsplashRead here.
One of the most common mistakes during an incident is assuming that the operating system isn’t telling you what happened.
When an application crashes unexpectedly, authentication starts failing, latency increases, or a service becomes unavailable, engineers often begin by restarting processes, reviewing application logs, or rolling back deployments.
Those actions may restore service.
They rarely explain why the incident occurred.
Linux records an enormous amount of operational evidence long before an outage becomes visible to users. The challenge is rarely collecting more logs — it’s knowing which ones to correlate and in what order.
The incidents that take the longest to resolve are often the ones where the evidence was available from the beginning but scattered across different logging layers.
Learning how those layers fit together fundamentally changes how you investigate production systems.
Stop Treating Logs as Independent Sources
Most troubleshooting workflows still begin with:
tail -f application.log
